title: Review of New York Fed Studies on the Effects of Post-Crisis Banking Reforms

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed released an article in the Economic Policy Review surveying 12 studies from a project completed in June 2018 examining effects of post-crisis regulatory reforms on banking institutions. The review found that many findings aligned with theoretical predictions or earlier studies, yet some results contrasted with academic literature. Overall, banks did respond actively to new regulations, suggesting further research on additional outcomes would be valuable.
key_points: New York Fed completed a multi-year project in June 2018 examining effects of post-crisis regulatory reforms on banking institutions
The review covers 12 individual studies focused on bank risk-taking, funding costs, profitability, and liquidity impacts
Authors are Richard K. Crump (assistant vice president, Capital Markets Function) and João A. C. Santos (senior vice president, Financial Intermediation Function)
Published in the Economic Policy Review, the New York Fed's policy-oriented journal on macroeconomic and financial market topics
Studies showed banks actively responded to new regulations
Some study findings contrasted with evidence in the academic literature on banking reforms
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical grounding for understanding how post-crisis banking regulations have affected bank behavior and market structure. The findings are relevant for policymakers evaluating whether current regulatory frameworks achieve their intended goals of financial stability without unduly constraining credit availability or bank profitability.
market_structure_context: Research examines structural changes to banking sector from post-crisis reforms including effects on bank risk profiles, how regulations altered funding costs for institutions, changes in bank profitability metrics, and impacts on market liquidity provisions by banks.

issues_found: Source is a press release announcing a research paper, not the full paper itself - contains only executive summary-level content without substantive findings
event_type set to 'research_paper' but source_type indicates 'blog_post' - source is technically a press release that summarizes 12 studies rather than presenting original research
The summary rearranges the order of claims from the source text (places 'banks responded actively' before the alignment/contrast findings) but does not contradict the source
Age: October 2018 source is over 7.5 years old relative to TODAY (2026-04-10), though still within archival value window for historical regulatory analysis

human_review.notes: Record is mostly accurate with no critical factual errors. The primary concern is source thinness (press release vs. full research paper), but this is acknowledged in the record. Key points are well-supported, event_type classification is reasonable, and the domain quality (ny.frb.org) supports acceptance at borderline confidence.

notes: This is a historical record (2018) announcing research that may be of interest for regulatory impact analysis. The source itself is thin — it describes what the 12 studies examined but doesn't present detailed findings. The actual research papers are available on Liberty Street Economics as a blog series. SOURCE METADATA TARGET says 'market structure' and the content is consistent with that classification as it discusses banking reforms affecting market structure.
